facade_colour: red-orange brick with buff/yellow brick quoins, voussoirs, and banding

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Row, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a row of three, they retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, brick cladding with polychromatic brickwork, first storey bay windows, and fenestration pattern with round and segmentally arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: Local | Residential | Transition to Bellevue Park

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: red-orange brick with buff/yellow brick quoins, voussoirs, and banding
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Good condition, polychromatic brickwork well-preserved, some vegetation growing on facade
photo_observations.window_details: Dark-framed windows, second floor window in gable area, windows with segmental and round arched heads
photo_observations.door_details: Dark front door beside bay window, accessed via steps
photo_observations.cornice_details: Dark painted eave with gable trim
photo_observations.bay_window_details: First floor three-sided projecting bay with brick construction and segmental arched windows
photo_observations.porch_details: Simple entry stoop with steps, no covered porch
photo_observations.decorative_details: Buff brick quoins at corners, polychromatic brick banding, brick voussoirs over windows (segmental and round arched), decorative brickwork in gable
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Evergreen tree partially obstructing facade view, otherwise appears largely original
photo_observations.notes: Bay-and-gable row house with excellent polychromatic brickwork including round and segmental arched openings as documented in HCD statement. Part of a row of three (137-139-141).

facade_description: 141 Denison Ave presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, row, bay-and-gable typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double hung, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows, string courses, quoins, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a row of three, they retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, brick cladding with polychromatic brickwork, first storey bay windows, and fenestration pattern with round and segmentally arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
